rawHead:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Class field theory — reciprocity for abelian extensions</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  .chip{display:inline-block;min-width:2em;text-align:center;padding:2px 7px;
    border-radius:4px;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.88rem;margin:1px;
    background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);color:var(--ink)}
  .chip.cls0{background:#1a2418;color:var(--green);border-color:#2a4a20}
  .chip.cls1{background:#2a1a28;color:var(--pink);border-color:#4a2840}
  .chip.cls2{background:#2a241a;color:var(--yellow);border-color:#4a3a20}
  .chip.cls3{background:#1a2030;color:var(--blue);border-color:#2a3852}
  .chip.cls4{background:#221a2a;color:var(--violet);border-color:#3a2a4a}
  .dict{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:.7rem;margin:.5rem 0}
  .dict .cell{background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;padding:.55rem .7rem;font-size:.92rem}
  .dict .cell.hd{color:var(--mute);text-transform:uppercase;font-size:.72rem;letter-spacing:.12em;background:transparent;border:none;padding:.1rem .2rem}
  .tower{display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;gap:.3rem;margin:.4rem 0}
  .tower .lvl{background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.35rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;min-width:11em;text-align:center}
  .tower .lvl.hi{color:var(--yellow);border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .tower .edge{color:var(--mute);font-size:.75rem;line-height:1}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Motivation 2 Class group 3 Hilbert class field 4 Artin map 5 Ray class fields 6 Existence theorem 7 Conductor−discriminant 8 Quadratic reciprocity 9 Beyond abelian

Class field theory

Every abelian extension of a number field is controlled by a purely internal invariant of the field — its ray class groups. From Kronecker–Weber at the bottom to the idèle-class reciprocity at the top, CFT is the first crossing of arithmetic and Galois that works in full generality.

blocks.html:
1. Motivation: reciprocity for abelian extensions

Algebraic number theory begins with the question: given a polynomial $f(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$, for which primes $p$ does it split into linear factors modulo $p$? When $f$ has abelian Galois group, the answer is astonishingly clean: it depends only on congruence conditions on $p$.

The archetype is the cyclotomic case. The $n$-th cyclotomic field $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ is Galois over $\mathbb{Q}$ with

$$ \Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}) \;\cong\; (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times, \qquad \sigma_a(\zeta_n)=\zeta_n^{a}. $$

A prime $p\nmid n$ is unramified, and its Frobenius is exactly the class $\sigma_p\in(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$. So the splitting of $p$ depends only on $p\bmod n$ — a congruence law. The Kronecker–Weber theorem says this is the whole story over $\mathbb{Q}$:

Kronecker–Weber. Every finite abelian extension $L/\mathbb{Q}$ is contained in some cyclotomic field $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$. Equivalently, the maximal abelian extension $\mathbb{Q}^{\mathrm{ab}}=\bigcup_n \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$.

Class field theory is the generalization to an arbitrary number field $K$: abelian extensions of $K$ are governed not by $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$, but by the ray class groups of $K$. Before we state that, we need the (ordinary) class group.

params.title: Cyclotomic tower & abelian subfields
params.hint: pick $n$ to see $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ and its subfields

layout.html: Every abelian extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ is a subfield of some $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ (Kronecker–Weber). Subfields of $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ correspond to subgroups of $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$.

params.title: Cyclotomic tower & abelian subfields
params.hint: pick $n$ to see $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ and its subfields

layout.html: Every abelian extension of $\mathbb{Q}$ is a subfield of some $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ (Kronecker–Weber). Subfields of $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ correspond to subgroups of $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$.

blocks.html:
2. The ideal class group

Let $K$ be a number field with ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$. Not every $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a UFD: $\mathcal{O}_K = \mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ has $6 = 2\cdot 3 = (1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$ with no common refinement by irreducibles. The remedy, due to Dedekind, is to replace numbers by ideals.

A fractional ideal of $K$ is a nonzero $\mathcal{O}_K$-submodule $I\subset K$ such that $dI\subset\mathcal{O}_K$ for some $d\in\mathcal{O}_K$. The fractional ideals form a free abelian group $I_K$ under multiplication, generated by the prime ideals. The principal fractional ideals $P_K=\{(\alpha):\alpha\in K^\times\}$ form a subgroup. The quotient

$$ \operatorname{Cl}(K) \;=\; I_K / P_K $$

is the ideal class group. It is finite, its order is the class number $h_K$, and $h_K=1$ precisely when $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID (equivalently a UFD). So $\operatorname{Cl}(K)$ is a numerical measure of the failure of unique factorization.

Example. $K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, $\mathcal{O}_K = \mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$, class number $2$. The non-trivial class is represented by $\mathfrak{p}=(2,1+\sqrt{-5})$. Indeed $\mathfrak{p}^2 = (2)$ — the square is principal, so $[\mathfrak{p}]^2 = 1$ in $\operatorname{Cl}(K)$.

For imaginary quadratic fields $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ with $d<0$, the class number is computable and small for small $|d|$. A classical result (Heegner, Stark): there are exactly nine imaginary-quadratic fields with $h_K=1$, namely $d=-1,-2,-3,-7,-11,-19,-43,-67,-163$.

params.title: Class groups of imaginary-quadratic $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$

layout.html: 12 34 ≥5

blocks.html:
See also
  • Reciprocity toward Artin maps

Used in
Class field theory · Artin reciprocity map
Class field theory · Hilbert class field

params.title: Class groups of imaginary-quadratic $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$

layout.html: 12 34 ≥5

blocks.html:
3. The Hilbert class field

Class groups would be a curious but isolated invariant — except that they are Galois groups. Specifically, associated to every number field $K$ there is a distinguished abelian extension, the Hilbert class field $H$, characterized as follows.

Theorem (Furtwängler, Artin). There exists a unique finite abelian extension $H/K$ with:
  1. every prime of $K$ is unramified in $H$ (and the infinite places are also unramified);
  2. $H$ is maximal with this property;
  3. the Artin map gives an isomorphism $\operatorname{Cl}(K) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gal(H/K)$.
In particular $[H:K] = h_K$.

So the class group of $K$ is literally the Galois group of a canonical abelian unramified cover of $K$. The "deformed" unique factorization in $K$ becomes honest unique factorization in $H$ (for ideals coming from $K$): any ideal $\mathfrak{a}$ of $\mathcal{O}_K$ becomes principal when extended to $\mathcal{O}_H$ (this is the principal ideal theorem).

Worked example: $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$

We have $h_K=2$, so $[H:K]=2$ and $H$ is a quadratic extension of $K$, everywhere unramified, and quadratic, biquadratic, or cyclic over $\mathbb{Q}$. Computation (or a table) gives

$$ H \;=\; K(\sqrt{-1}) \;=\; \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5}, i) \;=\; \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-1},\sqrt{5}). $$

Note $H/\mathbb{Q}$ is biquadratic of degree $4$, abelian with Galois group $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$, and contains the cyclotomic $\mathbb{Q}(i,\sqrt{5}) \subset \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{20})$, consistent with Kronecker–Weber. The ramified primes of $H/\mathbb{Q}$ are exactly the ramified primes of $K/\mathbb{Q}$ (namely $2$ and $5$): no new ramification has been introduced.

params.title: Hilbert class field tower

blocks.html:
See also
  • Class group and units

Used in
Elliptic curves · The j-invariant and isomorphism classes
Quadratic forms & genus theory · Forms and ideal classes

params.title: Hilbert class field tower

blocks.html:
4. The Artin (reciprocity) map

Let $L/K$ be a finite abelian extension of number fields, and let $\mathfrak{p}$ be a prime of $K$ unramified in $L$. Choose any prime $\mathfrak{P}$ of $L$ above $\mathfrak{p}$. The decomposition group $D_\mathfrak{P}\subset\Gal(L/K)$ has a canonical generator, the Frobenius $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}$, characterized by

$$ \operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}(x) \equiv x^{N\mathfrak{p}} \pmod{\mathfrak{P}}\quad\text{for all } x\in\mathcal{O}_L, $$

where $N\mathfrak{p} = \#(\mathcal{O}_K/\mathfrak{p})$ is the absolute norm. Because $L/K$ is abelian, conjugation of $\mathfrak{P}$ by $\Gal(L/K)$ leaves the decomposition group unchanged, so $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}$ depends only on $\mathfrak{p}$. We write $\bigl(\tfrac{L/K}{\mathfrak{p}}\bigr)$ or $\operatorname{Frob}_\mathfrak{p}$.

Extending multiplicatively to ideals prime to the ramified set yields a homomorphism

$$ \Big(\,\tfrac{L/K}{\cdot}\,\Big):\; I_K^{S} \;\longrightarrow\; \Gal(L/K), $$

where $S$ is the (finite) set of ramified primes.

Artin reciprocity (main theorem, conductor form). For every finite abelian $L/K$ there is a modulus $\mathfrak{m}$ (its conductor) supported on $S\cup\{\infty\}$ such that the Artin map factors through the ray class group $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)$ and gives a surjection $$ \operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)\twoheadrightarrow \Gal(L/K), $$ with kernel generated by the classes of primes that split completely in $L$. In particular the Frobenius of $\mathfrak{p}$ depends only on the class of $\mathfrak{p}$ in $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K)$.

For the Hilbert class field ($\mathfrak{m}=1$) this recovers the isomorphism $\operatorname{Cl}(K)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Gal(H/K)$. For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$, the conductor is $(n)\cdot\infty$ and the Artin map is $p\mapsto\sigma_p$, $\sigma_p(\zeta_n)=\zeta_n^p$ — the cyclotomic reciprocity law.

params.title: Artin-map box
params.hint: input a prime $p$; read off its Frobenius

layout.html: The Artin map factors through a ray class group. For $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}$ the conductor is $n\cdot\infty$, so $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ depends only on $p\bmod n$.

blocks.html:
Used in
Class field theory · Conductor-discriminant formula
Class field theory · Local and global class field theory

params.title: Artin-map box
params.hint: input a prime $p$; read off its Frobenius

layout.html: The Artin map factors through a ray class group. For $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}$ the conductor is $n\cdot\infty$, so $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ depends only on $p\bmod n$.

blocks.html:
5. Ray class fields, conductors, idèles

The Hilbert class field controls unramified abelian extensions. To capture all abelian extensions, we allow a controlled amount of ramification, tracked by a modulus.

Modulus and ray class group

A modulus $\mathfrak{m}$ of $K$ is a formal product $\mathfrak{m} = \mathfrak{m}_{\mathrm{f}}\cdot\mathfrak{m}_\infty$ where $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathrm{f}}\subset\mathcal{O}_K$ is an ideal and $\mathfrak{m}_\infty$ is a subset of the real embeddings of $K$. Define

  • $I_K^\mathfrak{m}$ = fractional ideals coprime to $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathrm{f}}$;
  • $P_K^\mathfrak{m}$ = principal $(\alpha)$ with $\alpha \equiv 1 \pmod{\mathfrak{m}_{\mathrm{f}}}$ and $\alpha$ positive at every real place in $\mathfrak{m}_\infty$.

The ray class group modulo $\mathfrak{m}$ is $\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K) = I_K^\mathfrak{m}/P_K^\mathfrak{m}$. It is finite. For $\mathfrak{m}=1$ it recovers $\operatorname{Cl}(K)$; for $K=\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathfrak{m}=(n)\cdot\infty$, it is $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$.

Existence theorem. For every modulus $\mathfrak{m}$ of $K$ there is a unique abelian extension $K(\mathfrak{m})/K$ — the ray class field of conductor $\mathfrak{m}$ — with $$ \Gal(K(\mathfrak{m})/K)\;\cong\;\operatorname{Cl}_\mathfrak{m}(K). $$ Moreover every finite abelian $L/K$ sits inside $K(\mathfrak{m})$ for some (minimal) $\mathfrak{m}$, and this minimal modulus is the conductor $\mathfrak{f}(L/K)$. The conductor is supported exactly on the ramified primes.

Idèles — the modulus-free formulation

Taking inverse limits over all moduli, the ray class groups pack into a single topological group. Define the idèles

$$ \mathbb{A}_K^\times \;=\; \prod_v{}' K_v^\times $$

(restricted product over completions $K_v$, with $x_v\in\mathcal{O}_v^\times$ for almost all finite $v$), and the idèle-class group $C_K=\mathbb{A}_K^\times / K^\times$ (diagonal embedding). Then:

Artin reciprocity (idelic form). There is a continuous surjection $$ \theta_K\;:\; C_K \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \Gal(K^{\mathrm{ab}}/K), $$ with kernel the connected component of $1$. Finite abelian extensions $L/K$ correspond bijectively to finite-index open subgroups $N\subset C_K$, via $N = \ker\bigl(C_K\to\Gal(L/K)\bigr)$.

This packages Kronecker–Weber ($K=\mathbb{Q}$, $C_\mathbb{Q}/\text{connected}$ $\cong\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}^\times$), the Hilbert class field (all-ramified = $\mathcal{O}_K^\times$ kernel), and all ray class fields into one statement.

params.title: Ray class / conductor slider

layout.html: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, $\operatorname{Cl}_{n\infty}(\mathbb{Q})=(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$ and the ray class field of conductor $n\infty$ is $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$. Abelian $L/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor dividing $n$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $L\subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$.

blocks.html:
Used in
Class field theory · Takagi existence theorem

params.title: Ray class / conductor slider

layout.html: For $K=\mathbb{Q}$, $\operatorname{Cl}_{n\infty}(\mathbb{Q})=(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$ and the ray class field of conductor $n\infty$ is $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$. Abelian $L/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor dividing $n$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $L\subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$.

blocks.html:
6. Takagi's existence theorem

Artin reciprocity gives, for each abelian $L/K$, a surjection from a ray class group (or from the idèle class group $C_K$) onto $\Gal(L/K)$. Takagi's existence theorem provides the converse: it says the correspondence is a bijection, so any admissible congruence subgroup is actually the kernel of the Artin map for a genuine abelian extension.

Existence theorem (Takagi). Fix a number field $K$.
  • Classical form. For every modulus $\mathfrak{m}$ and every subgroup $H$ with $P_K^{\mathfrak{m}}\subseteq H\subseteq I_K^{\mathfrak{m}}$ of finite index, there is a unique finite abelian extension $L/K$, unramified outside $\mathfrak{m}$, with $H = \ker\!\bigl(I_K^{\mathfrak{m}}\to\Gal(L/K)\bigr)$. In particular $\Gal(L/K)\cong I_K^{\mathfrak{m}}/H$.
  • Idelic form. Finite abelian extensions $L/K$ are in bijection with finite-index open subgroups $N\subset C_K$, via $N=\ker\theta_{L/K}$. Under this bijection $L\subseteq L'\Leftrightarrow N\supseteq N'$, so the lattice of abelian extensions is anti-isomorphic to the lattice of open finite-index subgroups of $C_K$.

Combined with the reciprocity theorem, existence turns class field theory into an algorithm: to build the abelian extension of $K$ with Galois group $G$, find a finite-index subgroup $N\subset C_K$ with $C_K/N\cong G$ and read off the fixed field. For $K=\mathbb{Q}$ this recovers Kronecker–Weber — every finite-index $N\subset C_\mathbb{Q}$ containing the connected component cuts out a subfield of some $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$ — and for imaginary quadratic $K$ it underlies the theory of complex multiplication.

See also
  • Reciprocity toward Artin maps
  • The idèle class group

7. The conductor-discriminant formula

Every character $\chi:\Gal(L/K)\to\mathbb{C}^\times$ of a finite abelian extension has an Artin conductor $\mathfrak{f}(\chi)\subset\mathcal{O}_K$: the smallest modulus through which $\chi$ factors on the ray class side, or equivalently the product over ramified primes $\mathfrak{p}$ of $\mathfrak{p}^{n(\chi,\mathfrak{p})}$ where $n(\chi,\mathfrak{p})$ measures how deeply $\chi$ restricts to the higher ramification filtration of $\mathfrak{p}$. The conductor of the extension itself is $\mathfrak{f}(L/K)=\operatorname{lcm}_\chi \mathfrak{f}(\chi)$.

The conductor-discriminant formula (Führerdiskriminantenproduktformel, Hasse) expresses the discriminant of $L/K$ as a product over characters:

$$ \mathfrak{d}_{L/K} \;=\; \prod_{\chi\in \widehat{\Gal(L/K)}} \mathfrak{f}(\chi). $$

So ramification in $L/K$ is controlled character-by-character: an unramified prime contributes to no conductor and to no factor of the discriminant, a tamely ramified prime $\mathfrak{p}$ contributes $\mathfrak{p}$ to exactly the $\chi$ that are non-trivial on inertia, and wildly ramified primes contribute with higher multiplicity via the Swan conductor. Specialising to $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}$ recovers the classical formula $\operatorname{disc}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}) = \prod_{p\mid n} p^{\,\varphi(n)(1-\tfrac{1}{p-1})/\cdots}$ as the product over Dirichlet characters mod $n$ of their individual conductors.

See also
  • Ideal factorization and ramification

8. Quadratic reciprocity as the baby case

Gauss's law of quadratic reciprocity sits inside class field theory as the simplest non-trivial instance of Artin reciprocity over $\mathbb{Q}$. Recall the Legendre symbol $\bigl(\tfrac{q}{p}\bigr)$ equals $+1$ if $q$ is a nonzero square mod $p$, $-1$ otherwise, and $0$ if $p\mid q$.

For a prime $q$, set $q^* = (-1)^{(q-1)/2} q$, so that $q^*\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q^*})$ is the unique quadratic subfield of $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_q)$. Then for any odd prime $p\ne q$:

Shape of the argument.
  • $\Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q^*})/\mathbb{Q})\cong\{\pm 1\}$.
  • $\operatorname{Frob}_p$ in this extension equals $+1$ iff $p$ splits, iff $q^*$ is a square mod $p$, iff $\bigl(\tfrac{q^*}{p}\bigr)=+1$.
  • But $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q^*})\subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_q)$, so by cyclotomic reciprocity $\operatorname{Frob}_p$ depends only on $p\bmod q$: it lands in the unique index-$2$ subgroup of $(\mathbb{Z}/q)^\times$, namely the squares.
  • Therefore $\bigl(\tfrac{q^*}{p}\bigr) = \bigl(\tfrac{p}{q}\bigr)$. Unwinding the sign of $q^*$ gives quadratic reciprocity.

In one line: the Artin map for $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q^*})/\mathbb{Q}$ factors through $(\mathbb{Z}/q)^\times$, and that factorization is quadratic reciprocity. The general Artin reciprocity theorem extends this idea to every abelian extension of every number field. See quadratic reciprocity for a dedicated exploration, and Frobenius and reciprocity for the general dictionary.

params.title: Quadratic reciprocity via Frobenius
Quadratic reciprocity via Frobenius

blocks.html:
9. What lies beyond — the Langlands program

Class field theory is a complete, beautiful description of the abelian quotient of the absolute Galois group. The full group $G_K = \Gal(\overline{K}/K)$ is very far from abelian, and its non-abelian quotients control such things as the splitting of non-solvable polynomials, points on modular curves, and the arithmetic of elliptic curves.

The Langlands program is the sweeping generalization: it predicts that all finite-dimensional continuous representations of $G_K$ correspond to certain analytic objects called automorphic representations. In one line:

params.bodyMarkup:
The reciprocity dictionary
abelian on the left, non-abelian on the right
Arithmetic side
Analytic / automorphic side
Abelian Galois extensions of $K$
Finite-order characters of $C_K = \mathbb{A}_K^\times/K^\times$ (Hecke characters)
1-dim Galois representations $\rho: G_K\to\mathbb{C}^\times$
Hecke characters $\chi: C_K\to\mathbb{C}^\times$
$\Leftrightarrow$ Class field theory
Artin $L$-function = Hecke $L$-function
Non-abelian $n$-dim Galois rep'ns $\rho: G_K\to \mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ or $\mathrm{GL}_n(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_\ell)$
Automorphic representations of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{A}_K)$
$\Leftrightarrow$ Langlands program (still open in general)
Artin $L$-function = automorphic $L$-function
2-dim Galois rep'ns over $\mathbb{Q}$ from elliptic curves
Weight-2 cusp forms (modularity theorem)
CFT is the abelian Langlands program. Everything beyond requires automorphic forms.

blocks.html:
Within this picture:

  • Abelian (CFT). $n=1$. Reciprocity map $C_K\to G_K^{\mathrm{ab}}$. Proved completely by the 1930s.
  • 2-dimensional, over $\mathbb{Q}$. Galois representations attached to modular forms, and in the other direction modularity of elliptic curves. See modular forms and elliptic curves. The modularity theorem for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ is a highly non-trivial special case.
  • General $n$. Still largely conjectural. Proved for $\mathrm{GL}_n$ over function fields (L. Lafforgue, V. Lafforgue), open over number fields.

For further exploration within the notebook, the closest siblings to CFT are

  • algebraic number theory — rings of integers, ideals, splitting of primes;
  • Galois theory — extensions and their groups;
  • quadratic reciprocity — the baby case, worked in detail;
  • Frobenius and reciprocity — the general dictionary of splitting;
  • $p$-adic numbers — the local completions that build the idèles;
  • Dirichlet series and Euler products — the $L$-functions on the analytic side.

params.title: Abelian vs non-abelian: what CFT and Langlands say
Abelian vs non-abelian: what CFT and Langlands say
